system_prompt: Tell me what category of waste the material in the image falls into (paper and cardboard, glass, plastic and metal containers, non-recyclable general waste, organic waste or compost, hazardous or electronic waste). Also give me a list of 4 ideas to reuse the object as crafts. Respond only in a json with classification example: {type: 'hazardous and electronic waste} ideas: [ideas and steps]'
in_this_lesson1: In this lesson, you will learn about why and what to recyle.
in_this_lesson2: In this lesson you will learn about the function of each type of bin
in_this_lesson3: In this lesson you will learn aboout the most common and important plastic symbols
reduce_the_amount: Reduce the Amount of Waste We Generate
reduce_text: Minimizing waste helps prevent overfilled landfills and reduces pollution from incineration, both of which release harmful substances like methane and toxins into the environment. Reusing items—such as containers, clothing, or packaging—can significantly cut the total volume of waste produced. A zero-waste strategy that emphasizes reuse could reduce greenhouse gas emissions equivalent to shutting down 80 coal power plants by 2030 (GAIA, 2019), highlighting the large-scale impact of small daily choices.
save_text: Reusing materials reduces the demand for virgin resources like wood, metals, sand, and fossil fuels. For example, recycling one ton of office paper saves over two tons of wood, while reusing aluminum avoids the need to mine bauxite and saves 95% of the energy used in primary production. By giving materials a second life, we reduce pressure on ecosystems and help preserve finite resources for future generations (EPA, 2023).
decrease_air: Decrease Air, Water, and Soil Pollution
decrease_text: The extraction and processing of raw materials release pollutants into air, water, and soil, threatening ecosystems and human health. Reusing items reduces the need for these activities, cutting emissions and chemical waste. Recycling paper, for instance, produces 74% less air pollution and 35% less water pollution compared to making it from raw pulp (Environmental Paper Network, 2018), while reuse of plastics helps prevent microplastic contamination of natural environments.
avoid_extracting: Avoid Extracting and Processing New Resources by Reusing
avoid_text: Reusing glass, plastic, or metal avoids the environmental costs of mining, drilling, or refining. For instance, reusing glass cullet can save over 300 kg of CO₂ per ton and reduce energy used for melting by up to 30%. Lifecycle studies show that reuse reduces greenhouse gas emissions up to 88% more than recycling (Ellen MacArthur Foundation, 2021), making it one of the most effective strategies for sustainable consumption.
reduce_energy: Reduce Energy Consumption and Greenhouse Gas Emissions
reduce_energy_text: Reusing materials cuts the energy required for production, which in turn lowers greenhouse gas emissions. Producing recycled aluminum, for example, uses only 5% of the energy needed to make it from raw ore, and recycling glass and plastic saves significant electricity and fossil fuel use. In 2018, U.S. recycling and composting avoided 193 million metric tons of CO₂-equivalent emissions, and global reuse models could reduce emissions by up to 80% in key sectors (WRAP, 2022; EPA, 2023).
paper_and_cardboard_text: Newspapers, magazines, office paper, notebooks, cardboard and paper packaging.
glass_text: Glass bottles, glass jars, and other glass containers.
plastic_and_metal_text: Plastic bottles, aluminum cans, tetra paks, metal food tins.
general_waste_text: Food-contaminated items, diapers, broken ceramics or mirrors, styrofoam (in some regions).
organic_waste_text: Food scraps, coffee grounds, tea bags, yard trimmings, leaves and eggshells.
hazardous_waste_text: Batteries, electronics (phones, chargers), chemicals, paints, and other hazardous materials.
recyclable_inorganic_text: Plastic bottles and containers (clean), paper and cardboard, glass bottles and jars, aluminum cans and metal tins, Tetra Pak containers.
non_recyclable_inorganic_text: Cigarette butts, sanitary pads and diapers, ceramics, broken glass or mirrors, styrofoam (depending on municipality), candy/chip wrappers and laminated packaging.
organic_waste_text2: Food scraps (fruit and vegetable peels, leftovers), coffee grounds and tea bags, garden waste (leaves, small branches), eggshells.
hazardous_or_text: Batteries, electronic devices and cables, medicines and expired pharmaceuticals, paint, solvents, and chemicals.
plastic_only_text: Plastic bottles, plastic containers, plastic bags (if clean and allowed).
find_out: Find out what type of clasification is your waste and ideas of how to reuse it.
choose: Choose a game to review the topics from the information section.
memory_text: Match the trash with its recycling category
connect_text: Select and connect symbols with their description
clean_text: Answer questions to remove trash and save the ocean
connect_instructions: Click an image of a plastic type and drag it onto the correct description.
flexible_plastic_used: Flexible plastic used in bags, wraps and squeezable bottles. Sometimes collected at stores.
used_in_food: Used in food containers, caps and packaging. Durable and heat-resistant.
found_in_detergent: Found in detergent bottles, toys and piping. Strong and widely recyclable.
used_for_packaging: Used for packaging bottles, food containers and textiles. Widely recyclable.
used_in_plumbing: Used in plumbing, flooring and vinyl records. Rarely recycled.
micellaneous_plastics: Miscellaneous plastics (acrylic, polycarbonate, multilayers). Difficult to recycle.
found_in_disposable: Found in disposable plates, cups and foam trays. Rarely recycled.
you_matched: You matched all plastic types correctly.
clean_instructions: Answer questions correctly to remove trash and clean the ocean.
you_lost: You lost all your lives. Try again to save the ocean.
the_whales: The whales are happy thanks to your help.
